Marketing Strategy — BUSM 304

Being Big Isn't Always Best — Being Efficient Is What Really Counts

Meredith T. Villaluz Spring 20264 min read

When it comes to marketing, being big isn't always best — being efficient is what really counts! This week in my Principles of Marketing class at BYU–Hawaii, I examined company and marketing strategy, and one assignment completely changed how I see the numbers behind big brands.

Facebook vs Google — A Surprising Finding

I analyzed Facebook vs. Google using key metrics like marketing ROI, net marketing contribution, and marketing ROS — and discovered that Facebook is actually the more efficient marketer, despite Google having twice the sales!

Facebook vs Google — Marketing Efficiency
2×
Google's Sales vs Facebook
Higher
Facebook Marketing ROI
Better
Facebook Marketing ROS
Size ≠
Efficiency

The Strategic Framework Behind It

One of my biggest takeaways from Chapter 2 was learning how companies use the product/market development grid to grow in smart ways — whether through market penetration, market development, product development, or diversification. It's not only about more sales; it's about making thoughtful choices about where and how to grow.

I also realized that market segmentation and targeting allow companies to focus on their most valuable customer groups rather than trying to serve everyone. This focused strategy drives profitability — not just revenue.

"Whether you're reading a BCG growth-share matrix or figuring out your marketing mix, the lesson is the same: data plus strategy equals smarter marketing."
